perturbation.original_text: 1.8 "CPT Code" shall mean the American Medical Association's ("AMA") "Current Procedural Terminology" as published in the AMA's CPT Process Manual, Fourth Edition and any such future editions, for procedures used in performance of the Assay, and amounts reimbursed by Medicare for such procedures for location 99, as modified annually.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.8 "CPT Code" shall mean the Current Procedural Terminology for procedures used in performance of the Assay.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of "CPT Code" specified that it included amounts reimbursed by Medicare for procedures at location 99, as modified annually. By removing this clause, the definition becomes incomplete, particularly concerning reimbursement, which is a critical financial aspect of the agreement. The omission introduces ambiguity in Section 3.3(g), which relies on the CPT Code to determine billing and payment responsibilities, creating a contradiction in how reimbursements should be calculated and allocated.
perturbation.contradicted_text: (g) Billing, Reporting, Auditing. (i) In all cases where Life Technologies performs the Professional Component of the Assay, Life Technologies shall be responsible for billing the patient, the provider and/or the payer for the Test, including both the Technical Component and the Professional Component of the Assay, and the collection of such amounts with respect to each Test performed. Biocept shall bill Life Technologies directly once a month for the Technical Component of each Assay (including the cost for sample collection in accordance with Section 3.5(b)), based on pricing and reimbursement as agreed by the parties through the Joint Steering Committee within sixty (60) days of the Effective Date, generally based on each applicable CPT Code actually used in the performance of such Technical Component, employing the Medicare rates for the applicable year as described on Exhibit B for the initial one (1) year period, and Life Technologies shall pay Biocept within sixty (60) days following the invoice date. The parties shall disclose actual reimbursement for each Test, and shall reconcile or "true-up" any differences between the amounts actually received by Life Technologies for each billing item or code and amounts paid to Biocept on a quarterly basis. If the allocation of reimbursement is ambiguous with respect to billing codes or a Technical Component/Professional Component split, amounts received by Life Technologies that differ from the amounts agreed by the parties, or Medicare rates, shall be shared by the parties on the same ratio as the Technical Component/Professional Component ratio for Medicare. The Medicare rates used by the parties as the basis for determining the amount Life Technologies will pay Biocept for the Technical Component of the Assay before the quarterly true-up will be adjusted annually at the beginning of the calendar year to reflect changes to such Medicare rates. Should Medicare change the basis for reimbursement of the Assay, the parties shall agree to negotiate a structure for revenue sharing that generally accomplishes the result achieved above. Both parties agree to strictly adhere to all applicable Laws with respect to billing practices. (ii) This Section 3.3(g) shall survive any termination or expiration of this Agreement for at least twelve (12) months following the effective date of such termination or expiration.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the specific reference to Medicare reimbursement rates and location 99, which the original text included in the definition of 'CPT Code'. Section 3.3(g) explicitly relies on these Medicare rates (as detailed on Exhibit B) for calculating payments for the Technical Component of the Assay. The omission of this reference creates a contradiction because it introduces ambiguity about the basis for reimbursement calculations, directly impacting the payment responsibilities outlined in Section 3.3(g). Without a clear definition tied to Medicare rates, the section's intended payment structure becomes unclear and unenforceable, leading to a direct conflict.

perturbation.original_text: 1.20 "Third Party(ies)" shall mean any entity other than Biocept or Life Technologies or an Affiliate of Biocept or Life Technologies.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.20 "Third Party(ies)" shall mean any entity other than Biocept or Life Technologies.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting "or an Affiliate of Biocept or Life Technologies" from the definition of "Third Party(ies)," potential conflicts arise regarding Intellectual Property Rights licensed from Affiliates. Specifically, Section 8.4, which discusses technology licenses from Third Parties, becomes ambiguous. It is no longer clear whether licenses from Affiliates are subject to the same cost allocation negotiations as those from external Third Parties, creating a contradiction about how to treat Affiliate-owned IP used in the Assay. This change generates uncertainty regarding whether agreements made with Affiliates must adhere to the same licensing and expense parameters as those made with true outside Third Parties.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8.4 Technology Licenses. To the extent that any Third Party Intellectual Property Rights related to the capture and detection of CTCs must be licensed to perform the Assay, such royalty shall be paid by Biocept. To the extent that either party owns Intellectual Property Rights to specific biomarkers, targets, kits, dyes or technology utilized in the Assay other than for the capture and detection of CTCs, it will, to the extent it is able, grant during the Term of the Agreement, a non-exclusive license to the other party to practice these Intellectual Property Rights for the Assay. To the extent that either party has licensed or will license Intellectual Property Rights from Third Parties related to specific biomarkers, targets, kits, dyes or technology utilized in the Assay other than for the capture and detection of CTCs, it will, to the extent it is able, grant, during the Term of the Agreement, a non-exclusive license to the other party, or ensure that the other party is covered under its license, to practice these Intellectual Property Rights for the Assay. In the event of the foregoing, then, subject to Section 8.5, the parties agree to negotiate in good faith an allocation of expenses for such Third Party licenses directly associated with the Assay.
perturbation.justification: The changed text, by omitting 'or an Affiliate of Biocept or Life Technologies' from the definition of 'Third Party(ies),' directly contradicts Section 8.4. Section 8.4 refers to 'Third Party Intellectual Property Rights'. The original definition included Affiliates as distinct from Third Parties. The change now makes it ambiguous whether Section 8.4 applies to licenses from Affiliates. This creates a conflict as it's no longer clear if the expense allocation negotiation process outlined in Section 8.4 applies to licenses from Affiliates, as they are now included in the definition of third parties, potentially changing the obligations regarding intellectual property licensing and expense allocation.

perturbation.original_text: 3.5 Joint Responsibilities. The parties shall use commercially reasonable efforts to cooperate and collaborate to develop the market for the Tests in the Territory. Without limiting the generality of the foregoing, the parties shall collaborate to provide the following: (a) Test Development. The parties shall mutually agree on the content and composition of Phase II of the Assay, and any Collaboration Assays as defined in Section 3.5(f), including specific analytes to be included in the Assay. Consideration for selection of analytes shall include medical need, clinical utility, technical feasibility, costs, reimbursement, and intellectual property status, e.g., the need for Third Party licenses to specific analytes. The parties shall agree on the Phase II Assay content at least six (6) months before anticipated Launch.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.5 Joint Responsibilities. The parties shall use commercially reasonable efforts to cooperate and collaborate to develop the market for the Tests in the Territory. Without limiting the generality of the foregoing, the parties shall collaborate to provide the following: (a) Test Development. The parties shall mutually agree on the content and composition of Phase II of the Assay, and any Collaboration Assays as defined in Section 3.5(f), including specific analytes to be included in the Assay. Consideration for selection of analytes shall include medical need, clinical utility, technical feasibility, costs, reimbursement, and intellectual property status, e.g., the need for Third Party licenses to specific analytes.
perturbation.explanation: The requirement that "The parties shall agree on the Phase II Assay content at least six (6) months before anticipated Launch" is omitted, this weakens the overall agreement. The omission introduces ambiguity into Section 3.5(a) regarding the Phase II Assay agreement deadline. This impacts planning and execution. Removing the timeline potentially complicates the Joint Steering Committee's duties, leaving the project open ended with respect to approval of the Test content and creation of Test, thereby making the project open ended.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 4.2 Duties. The Joint Steering Committee shall meet in person or by teleconference or videoconference no less than monthly during the Term or as otherwise mutually agreed by the parties from time to time, with attendees other than Joint Steering Committee members permitted to participate in or observe the meetings. The Joint Steering Committee shall be responsible for (a) monitoring the progress of the Collaboration, including discussions relating to Collaboration Assays, (b) physician education with respect to the Tests, (c) marketing, sales and account coordination, (d) any regulatory inquiries or requirements and other issues that affect the availability of the Tests, and (e) reimbursement issues (including annual review of relevant CPT Codes and changes thereto), logistical considerations, and other topics as necessary. The Joint Steering Committee shall serve as the principal forum for each party to (i) keep the other party informed of the results of its Collaboration activities; (ii) to discuss Test commercialization strategies, and (iii) generally to encourage and facilitate ongoing cooperation between the parties with respect to the Collaboration, including the business relationship and/or any other matter relating to the Collaboration and resolving disputes between the parties with respect to Intellectual Property Rights; provided, however, that (A) nothing in this Agreement shall limit either party's right to seek immediate equitable or injunctive relief where appropriate without any obligation to first submit the dispute to the Joint Steering Committee; and (B) any decision concerning medical necessity and patient care with respect to Test sold by or performed on behalf of the parties shall be the responsibility of each party's Medical Director, with the two Medical Directors working together to coordinate efforts and address concerns.
perturbation.justification: The omission of the 'six (6) months before anticipated Launch' deadline in Section 3.5(a) introduces a significant ambiguity regarding the timing of Phase II Assay content agreement. This contradicts the Joint Steering Committee's duties outlined in Section 4.2, which include 'monitoring the progress of the Collaboration' and 'discussions relating to Collaboration Assays.' Without a defined deadline, the Committee's ability to effectively monitor progress and facilitate discussions is impaired, creating a direct conflict between the modified text and the contradicted text.
